title: Two-Way Prepositions
summary: Nine prepositions that take either accusative (movement/direction) or dative (location/position).

sections.heading: The Nine Two-Way Prepositions
body: Nine German prepositions can govern either the accusative or the dative case depending on the meaning of the sentence.
The key question to ask is: Wohin? (Where to? → direction → accusative) or Wo? (Where? → location → dative).
Accusative (Wohin?) signals movement or change of position toward a destination. Dative (Wo?) signals a static position or location with no change of place.

The nine prepositions are: an (at/on – vertical surfaces or edges), auf (on top of), hinter (behind), in (in/into), neben (next to/beside), über (over/above), unter (under/below), vor (in front of/before), zwischen (between).
Common contractions: in + dem = im, in + das = ins, an + dem = am, an + das = ans.

examples.de: Ich lege das Buch auf den Tisch.
examples.en: I put the book onto the table. (Wohin? → accusative)

examples.de: Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch.
examples.en: The book is lying on the table. (Wo? → dative)

examples.de: Sie hängt das Bild an die Wand.
examples.en: She hangs the picture on the wall. (Wohin? → accusative)

examples.de: Das Bild hängt an der Wand.
examples.en: The picture is hanging on the wall. (Wo? → dative)
We are going to the cinema. (Wohin? → accusative: in + das)
We are at the cinema. (Wo? → dative: in + dem)

sections.heading: Common Verb Pairs: Putting vs. Being Located
body: German has paired verbs that correspond to the accusative/dative distinction: one verb describes an action that causes a change of position (requires accusative), the other describes the resulting static state (requires dative).
legen (to lay/put – lying flat) / liegen (to lie/be lying): 'Ich lege das Heft auf den Tisch.' → 'Das Heft liegt auf dem Tisch.'
stellen (to place upright) / stehen (to stand/be standing): 'Sie stellt die Flasche in den Kühlschrank.' → 'Die Flasche steht im Kühlschrank.'
hängen (to hang – transitive, someone hangs it) / hängen (to hang – intransitive, it is hanging): 'Er hängt den Kalender an die Wand.' → 'Der Kalender hängt an der Wand.'
setzen (to set/place/sit someone down) / sitzen (to sit/be sitting): 'Die Mutter setzt das Kind auf den Stuhl.' → 'Das Kind sitzt auf dem Stuhl.'

The verbs in the left column (legen, stellen, hängen-transitive, setzen) are all transitive — they take a direct object and require accusative after the two-way preposition. The verbs in the right column (liegen, stehen, hängen-intransitive, sitzen) describe states and require dative.

examples.de: Ich stelle die Vase auf den Tisch.
examples.en: I place the vase on the table. (stellen + accusative)

examples.de: Die Vase steht auf dem Tisch.
examples.en: The vase is standing on the table. (stehen + dative)

examples.de: Er legt das Buch neben das Heft.
examples.en: He lays the book next to the notebook. (legen + accusative)

examples.de: Das Buch liegt neben dem Heft.
examples.en: The book is lying next to the notebook. (liegen + dative)

examples.de: Sie setzt sich auf den Sessel.
examples.en: She sits down on the armchair. (sich setzen + accusative)
She is sitting on the armchair. (sitzen + dative)

cells: to place upright / to be standing

exercises.question: Ich hänge das Bild ___ die Wand. (Which case is needed and why?)
options: der Wand – dative, because the picture is already there
die Wand – accusative, because we are describing movement / placing
der Wand – dative, because 'an' always takes dative
dem Wand – dative, because hängen requires dative
exercises.explanation: 'Hänge' (transitive) describes the action of putting the picture on the wall (Wohin?), so 'an' takes the accusative: 'an die Wand'.

exercises.prompt: Die Katze sitzt ___ dem Sofa. (auf / in / an)
exercises.explanation: 'Auf' means 'on top of'. The cat is sitting on top of the sofa (Wo? → dative 'dem Sofa'). 'Auf dem Sofa' is correct.
'Stellen' is an action verb (Wohin?), so 'in' takes the accusative: 'in den Kühlschrank' (der Kühlschrank → accusative = den Kühlschrank).

exercises.question: Which sentence correctly uses the dative to show location?
options: Das Auto steht vor das Haus.
Das Auto steht vor dem Haus.
Das Auto stellt vor dem Haus.
Das Auto fährt vor dem Haus.
exercises.explanation: 'Steht' (stehen) describes a static location (Wo?), so 'vor' takes the dative: 'vor dem Haus'. 'Vor das Haus' would indicate movement (Wohin?).

exercises.prompt: Er legt das Hemd ___ den Schrank. (in / im)
exercises.explanation: 'Legen' is an action verb (Wohin? – placing into the wardrobe), so 'in' requires the accusative: 'in den Schrank'. 'Im' is a dative contraction (in + dem) used for location.
